# ILLUME — The Science of Agentic Scaling

> ILLUME builds the predictive science of multi-agent AI: scaling laws that classify any agent team into hard-ceiling, sublinear, or linear regimes, predicting when adding agents helps, when it fails, and what it costs in tokens and energy.

The site presents peer-reviewed research on the Ringelmann effect in multi-agent LLM systems — a two-parameter scaling law for effective team size — and a controlled study of multi-agent debate failure modes. The goal is to move multi-agent AI from heuristic engineering to a predictive physics of interaction.

## Research

- [The Ringelmann Effect in Multi-Agent LLM Systems: A Scaling Law for Effective Team Size](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02646): A two-parameter scaling law that classifies any agent configuration into hard-ceiling, sublinear, or linear regimes, validated across 38 model × task × condition cells. (arXiv:2606.02646)
- [The Cost of Consensus: Isolated Self-Correction Prevails Over Unguided Homogeneous Multi-Agent Debate](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3786335.3813137): A controlled study of N=10 homogeneous debate teams across three open-weight models on GSM-Hard and MMLU-Hard, decomposing debate failure into three mechanistic pathways. CAIS '26 Industry Spotlight.

## Key findings

- Sycophantic conformity: agents adopt the majority answer up to 85.5% of the time.
- Contextual fragility: correct reasoning is destabilized at rates up to 70%.
- Debate costs 2.1–3.4× more tokens for equal or lower accuracy.
